intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid, continuous motion in data review over instant jumps between sections
facade.label: Smooth scrolling
facade.acceptImplies: Build scroll-based reveal of experiment progress; prioritize easing curves and momentum for browsing long data histories
facade.rejectImplies: Use instant tab/panel switches; optimize for quick scanning over continuous motion; favor snappy interaction over fluid transitions

evidence.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid, continuous motion in data review over instant jumps between sections
evidence.implication: Build scroll-based reveal of experiment progress; prioritize easing curves and momentum for browsing long data histories

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left sidebar navigation paired with card-based layout for individual experiments will balance quick access to nav with focused, scannable experiment summaries
facade.axisTargeted: sidebar vs tabs navigation + card density
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values persistent context and visual grouping; dashboard should use left nav + modular cards to reduce cognitive load
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer tabbed navigation or list-based layouts; persistent sidebars might feel constraining for health tracking where context-switching between experiments is frequent

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer metric cards with oversized KPIs over narrative summaries for health tracking
facade.label: Big numbers, small text
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should lead with visual hierarchy — prominent numbers (weight, sleep hours, streak count) with minimal supporting context
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard needs narrative framing — each metric embedded in a sentence or micro-story ('You slept 7.2 hours—your best week') rather than bare numbers

facade.hypothesis: A personal health dashboard benefits from gentle, approachable forms that feel less clinical and more human-centered
facade.axisTargeted: Rounded vs sharp — organic warmth vs medical precision
facade.label: Soft rounded cards
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers softer, more inviting visual language; may favor curves in buttons, cards, and containers throughout the interface
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers cleaner, more structured geometry; sharp edges and flat planes signal clarity and data-driven professionalism

facade.hypothesis: A friendly, playful tone with celebration of small wins will feel more motivating than clinical language for long-term health tracking
facade.label: Cheerful check-ins
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers warmth and encouragement over detached data presentation; voice should include positive reinforcement and personality
facade.rejectImplies: User values clarity and objectivity over emotional tone; dashboard should prioritize neutral, straightforward communication without motivational framing

facade.hypothesis: The user prefers organic, human-centered visual representations over clinical or technical charts — favoring curves, warm colors, and narrative-driven data display that feels inviting rather than sterile.
facade.label: Soft data visualization
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into flowing graphs, rounded cards, earthy color palettes, and illustrated elements. Avoid stark grids and cold data tables.
facade.rejectImplies: Consider colder, more minimal visual language — clean typography, high contrast, precise geometric charts. Data presentation should feel fast and neutral rather than cozy.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking experience
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer instant, precise alignment of data points over fluid, organic positioning
facade.label: Snap to grid
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard elements (charts, metrics, experiment cards) should lock into structured layouts with no easing—fast, clean transitions between states
facade.rejectImplies: Confirm that smooth, momentum-based motion and organic spacing better serve reviewing long-term health data; prioritize fluid transitions over grid-snap behavior

draft.title: Experiment Tracker Dashboard
draft.summary: A personal health dashboard for monitoring long-term experiments with visual progress tracking, experiment cards, and key metrics at a glance.
